does: This paper identifies and analyzes four concrete strategies for fostering industry-wide cooperation on AI safety: communicating risks and benefits of safety investments, engaging in technical collaboration on safety research, increasing transparency about safety practices, and creating incentive structures that reward safety standards. The authors diagnose a fundamental collective action problem where competitive market pressures incentivize individual AI companies to under-invest in safety relative to what would be socially optimal, and argue that these four strategies can mitigate this misalignment by making safety cooperation more attractive and feasible for firms operating in competitive environments.
connects: This work builds on game-theoretic and policy analyses of coordination failures in emerging technology industries, extending insights from environmental regulation, pharmaceuticals, and cybersecurity to the AI domain. It responds to growing concerns about race dynamics in AI development and engages with earlier literature on how regulatory frameworks and industry standards can overcome collective action problems. The paper also connects to broader research on responsible AI governance and the role of transparency, technical standards, and stakeholder communication in reducing risks from advanced systems.
enables: This research provides a policy roadmap for regulators, industry bodies, and civil society actors seeking to construct cooperative safety governance structures before competitive pressures fully consolidate. It lays groundwork for designing practical incentive mechanisms—such as safety certifications, liability frameworks, or public commitments—that could make cooperation individually rational for firms. The analysis points toward future work on measuring cooperation effectiveness, identifying which strategies work best in different regulatory contexts, and building institutional mechanisms that sustain safety collaboration as AI capabilities advance.
